---
title: "TDAmapper.jl: A Complete Guide"
---

::: {.callout-note}
This tutorial assumes basic familiarity with Julia. No prior TDA knowledge is required.
:::

## The Three-Stage Pipeline {#sec-pipeline}

Every mapper computation follows the same three-stage pipeline:

```
mapper(X, cover, refiner, nerve)
     ↓              ↓             ↓
  Stage 1       Stage 2        Stage 3
  Cover         Refine         Nerve graph
  ─────────     ──────────     ───────────
  Partition     Cluster each   Nodes = clusters
  data into     cover element  Edges = overlap
  overlapping   with a chosen
  bins          algorithm
```

**Cover** — partition the data into overlapping groups via a filter $f: X \to \mathbb{R}$.
**Refine** — cluster each group independently.
**Nerve** — one node per cluster; edge between any two nodes that share a point.

---

## Setup {#sec-setup}

```{julia}
using TDAplots, CairoMakie
using MetricSpaces.Datasets           # sphere, torus, …
using TDAmapper.ImageCovers           # R1Cover, R2Cover
using TDAmapper.IntervalCovers        # Uniform, QuantileCover, …
using TDAmapper.Refiners              # DBscan, KMeans, …
using TDAmapper.Nerves                # SimpleNerve, SimplicialNerve, …

import Random; Random.seed!(42)

# Helper: draw a mapper graph into a pre-existing Makie Axis.
# Returns the scatter plot object so the caller can attach a Colorbar.
function mapper_panel!(ax, g, positions, sizes, values;
                       colormap = :viridis, title = "", edge_color = :lightgray)
    n = length(positions)
    pts = eltype(positions)[]
    for i in 1:n, j in g.fadjlist[i]
        j ≤ i && continue
        push!(pts, positions[i], positions[j])
    end
    isempty(pts) || linesegments!(ax, pts; color = edge_color, linewidth = 1.5)
    lo, hi = extrema(values)
    lo ≈ hi && (lo -= 1.0; hi += 1.0)
    sc = scatter!(ax, positions; markersize = sizes, color = values,
                  colormap = colormap, colorrange = (lo, hi))
    ax.title = title
    hidedecorations!(ax; label = false)
    hidespines!(ax)
    return sc
end
```

---

## 1. Your First Mapper {#sec-first}

We start with a classic: 1 000 points on a noisy circle.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Left: the raw point cloud, coloured by x-coordinate. Right: its Mapper graph."

X = sphere(1_000)           # 1000 points on a noisy unit circle in 2D
fv = first.(X)              # filter = x-coordinate

# Build the mapper
C  = R1Cover(fv, Uniform(length=10, expansion=0.3))
M  = classical_mapper(X, C, DBscan(radius=0.1))

println("Nodes: ", length(M.C), "   Edges: ", length(M.g.fadjlist))

# Side-by-side: raw cloud + mapper graph
fig = Figure(size=(900, 380))

# Left: scatter of the raw data
ax1 = Axis(fig[1, 1], title="Point cloud (n=1 000)")
scatter!(ax1, [p[1] for p in X], [p[2] for p in X];
         color=fv, colormap=:viridis, markersize=4)
hidedecorations!(ax1); hidespines!(ax1)

# Right: mapper graph (Spring layout, nodes sized by cluster size)
pos   = layout_spring(M)
sizes = rescale(map(length, M.C); min=12, max=55)
ax2   = Axis(fig[1, 2], title="Mapper graph")
sc    = mapper_panel!(ax2, M.g, pos, sizes, node_colors(M, fv); title="")
Colorbar(fig[1, 3], sc; label="x-coordinate")

fig
```

The mapper recovers the circular shape of the data: the graph is a cycle.

---

## 2. The Real Power: Same Nodes, Different Colors {#sec-colors}

The mapper graph is a fixed skeleton. The colours are *data you project onto it*.
The same set of nodes can answer many different questions simply by changing what quantity you average per node.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Four views of the same mapper graph, each coloured by a different quantity. The skeleton (positions and edges) is identical in all four panels."

# Four per-point quantities
fv_x   = [p[1] for p in X]
fv_y   = [p[2] for p in X]
fv_r   = [sqrt(p[1]^2 + p[2]^2) for p in X]           # distance from origin
fv_ang = [atan(p[2], p[1]) * 180 / π for p in X]       # angle in degrees

quantities = [
    (node_colors(M, fv_x),   :RdBu,    "x-coordinate"),
    (node_colors(M, fv_y),   :plasma,  "y-coordinate"),
    (node_colors(M, fv_r),   :viridis, "radius"),
    (node_colors(M, fv_ang), :hsv,     "angle (°)"),
]

# Freeze positions so all panels are spatially aligned
pos   = layout_spring(M)
sizes = rescale(map(length, M.C); min=12, max=55)

fig = Figure(size=(1000, 900))
for (k, (vals, cmap, label)) in enumerate(quantities)
    row, col = divrem(k - 1, 2)
    ax = Axis(fig[row + 1, 2col + 1], title=label)
    sc = mapper_panel!(ax, M.g, pos, sizes, vals; colormap=cmap)
    Colorbar(fig[row + 1, 2col + 2], sc)
end
fig
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The *x* and *y* panels show a smooth gradient cycling around the circle — confirming the graph is topologically $S^1$.
The *radius* panel is nearly uniform (all nodes ≈ 1 from the origin).
The *angle* panel shows the full $[-180°, 180°]$ range wrapping around.
:::

### Categorical coloring

When points carry labels (e.g. experimental groups, species, clusters from another method), use string vectors — TDAplots shows a legend automatically:

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Categorical coloring: upper vs lower half of the circle."

labels = [p[2] ≥ 0 ? "upper" : "lower" for p in X]

fig = mapper_plot(M;
    node_positions = pos,
    node_size      = sizes,
    node_values    = node_colors(M, labels))
fig
```

---

## 3. The Filter Function Shapes the Graph {#sec-filter}

The filter $f$ is the **most important design choice** — it determines which topological features you highlight. Here we compare three filters on a 3D torus.

```{julia}
X3 = torus(2_000)   # 2000 points on a 3D torus

fv_x3 = [p[1] for p in X3]   # left-right axis
fv_y3 = [p[2] for p in X3]   # depth axis
fv_z3 = [p[3] for p in X3]   # height axis

filters = [
    (fv_x3, "Filter: x-axis"),
    (fv_y3, "Filter: y-axis"),
    (fv_z3, "Filter: z-axis"),
]

fig = Figure(size=(1100, 360))
for (k, (flt, title)) in enumerate(filters)
    C_k = R1Cover(flt, Uniform(length=12, expansion=0.4))
    M_k = classical_mapper(X3, C_k, DBscan(radius=0.3))
    pos_k   = layout_spring(M_k)
    sizes_k = rescale(map(length, M_k.C); min=8, max=45)
    vals_k  = node_colors(M_k, flt)

    ax = Axis(fig[1, 2k-1], title=title)
    sc = mapper_panel!(ax, M_k.g, pos_k, sizes_k, vals_k; colormap=:hsv)
    Colorbar(fig[1, 2k], sc)
end
fig
```

The three filters slice the same torus differently, each revealing a different "hole":

- **x** and **y** filters produce a thick loop (one hole of the torus)
- **z** filter produces a thin loop (the other hole)

### Seeing the raw torus first

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "The torus coloured by z-coordinate. The Mapper graph (z-filter) summarises this structure as a circle."

fig = Figure(size=(600, 550))
ax = Axis3(fig[1, 1], title="Torus (colored by z)")
scatter!(ax,
    [p[1] for p in X3], [p[2] for p in X3], [p[3] for p in X3];
    color=fv_z3, colormap=:hsv, markersize=3)
Colorbar(fig[1, 2]; colormap=:hsv, colorrange=extrema(fv_z3), label="z")
fig
```

---

## 4. Clustering Algorithm Comparison {#sec-clustering}

The *refiner* controls how finely each bin is split. A coarser refiner produces fewer nodes; a finer one captures more local structure.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Same circle + same cover, four different clustering strategies. Node size encodes cluster membership count."

refiners = [
    (Trivial(),                       "Trivial\n(no clustering)"),
    (DBscan(radius=0.1),              "DBscan\n(ε=0.1)"),
    (DBscan(radius=0.3),              "DBscan\n(ε=0.3, coarser)"),
    (WardLinkage(threshold=0.3),      "Ward linkage\n(h=0.3)"),
]

C_base = R1Cover(fv, Uniform(length=10, expansion=0.35))

fig = Figure(size=(1000, 450))
for (k, (R_k, label)) in enumerate(refiners)
    M_k     = classical_mapper(X, C_base, R_k)
    pos_k   = layout_spring(M_k)
    sizes_k = rescale(map(length, M_k.C); min=8, max=50)
    vals_k  = node_colors(M_k, fv)

    col = 2k - 1
    ax = Axis(fig[1, col], title=label * "\n($(length(M_k.C)) nodes)")
    sc = mapper_panel!(ax, M_k.g, pos_k, sizes_k, vals_k; colormap=:RdBu)
    Colorbar(fig[1, col + 1], sc)
end
fig
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Trivial** treats each bin as one cluster (no refinement). DBscan with a small radius splits bins further — the number of nodes grows. A large DBscan radius merges nearby points and produces fewer nodes.
:::

---

## 5. Graph Layout Algorithms {#sec-layouts}

The layout algorithm controls where nodes are *drawn*, not the graph's topology. Three philosophically different strategies:

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "The same mapper graph placed by three different layout algorithms."

M_lay = classical_mapper(X, R1Cover(fv, Uniform(length=12, expansion=0.35)),
                         DBscan(radius=0.1))
vals_lay  = node_colors(M_lay, fv)
sizes_lay = rescale(map(length, M_lay.C); min=10, max=50)

layouts_named = [
    (layout_spring(M_lay),     "Spring\n(force-directed)"),
    (layout_landmarks(M_lay),  "Landmarks\n(preserves geometry)"),
    (layout_stress(M_lay),     "Stress\n(graph-distance aware)"),
]

fig = Figure(size=(1050, 380))
for (k, (pos_k, label)) in enumerate(layouts_named)
    ax = Axis(fig[1, 2k-1], title=label)
    sc = mapper_panel!(ax, M_lay.g, pos_k, sizes_lay, vals_lay; colormap=:viridis)
    Colorbar(fig[1, 2k], sc)
end
fig
```

The **Landmark** layout is particularly powerful: nodes are placed at the *centroid of their data points*, so the graph inherits the geometry of the original point cloud. For a circle, this layout makes the ring shape immediately visible.

### Metric-aware layouts

For higher-dimensional data, dimensionality-reduction layouts often produce cleaner pictures:

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "MDS layout places nodes according to centroid distances — often more interpretable for high-dimensional data."

pos_mds = layout_mds(M_lay)
fig = mapper_plot(M_lay;
    node_positions = pos_mds,
    node_values    = vals_lay,
    node_size      = sizes_lay,
    colormap       = :viridis)
fig
```

---

## 6. Ball Mapper {#sec-ball}

The ball mapper skips the filter entirely. Landmark points act as "centres" and an $\varepsilon$-ball around each one defines a cover element. It is faster and requires no domain knowledge about a good filter.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Ball mapper of the circle. Nodes are placed at the landmark coordinates (layout_landmarks)."

X_bm = sphere(2_000)
L    = farthest_points_sample_ids(X_bm, 80)   # 80 well-spread landmarks
M_bm = ball_mapper(X_bm, L, 0.25)

vals_bm  = node_colors(M_bm, [p[1] for p in X_bm])
sizes_bm = rescale(map(length, M_bm.C); min=8, max=40)

# layout_landmarks places each node at the centroid of its cover element
pos_bm = layout_landmarks(M_bm)

fig = mapper_plot(M_bm;
    node_positions = pos_bm,
    node_values    = vals_bm,
    node_size      = sizes_bm,
    colormap       = :RdBu)
fig
```

### Ball mapper on a torus

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Ball mapper of a 3D torus, coloured by z-coordinate. The circular structure is preserved."

X_bt  = torus(3_000)
L_bt  = farthest_points_sample_ids(X_bt, 150)
M_bt  = ball_mapper(X_bt, L_bt, 0.5)

fv_bt    = [p[3] for p in X_bt]
vals_bt  = node_colors(M_bt, fv_bt)
sizes_bt = rescale(map(length, M_bt.C); min=6, max=35)
pos_bt   = layout_landmarks(M_bt; dim=2)   # project 3D centroids → 2D

fig = mapper_plot(M_bt;
    node_positions = pos_bt,
    node_values    = vals_bt,
    node_size      = sizes_bt,
    colormap       = :hsv)
fig
```

---

## 7. 2D Mapper {#sec-2d}

When one filter is not enough, use two. The 2D mapper creates a grid of overlapping bins in 2D filter space — recovering structure that a single filter misses.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "2D mapper of the torus, using (x, z) as a joint filter. Coloured by z."

X_2d = torus(2_000)
f2   = p -> (p[1], p[3])    # x and z coordinates

M_2d = classical_mapper(X_2d, f2,
    Uniform(length=7, expansion=0.4),
    Uniform(length=7, expansion=0.4),
    DBscan(radius=0.3))

fv_2d    = [p[3] for p in X_2d]
vals_2d  = node_colors(M_2d, fv_2d)
sizes_2d = rescale(map(length, M_2d.C); min=5, max=40)
pos_2d   = layout_spring(M_2d)

println("2D mapper: ", length(M_2d.C), " nodes")

fig = mapper_plot(M_2d;
    node_positions = pos_2d,
    node_values    = vals_2d,
    node_size      = sizes_2d,
    colormap       = :hsv)
fig
```

---

## 8. Comparing Nerve Types {#sec-nerves}

The nerve controls which pairs of nodes are connected. A stricter nerve produces fewer (but more meaningful) edges.

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Same mapper, three different nerve strategies. Stricter nerves prune weak connections."

nerves = [
    (SimpleNerve(),    "SimpleNerve\n(any shared point)"),
    (MinCountNerve(5), "MinCountNerve\n(≥5 shared points)"),
    (PercentageNerve(0.25), "PercentageNerve\n(≥25% overlap)"),
]

C_n = R1Cover(fv, Uniform(length=12, expansion=0.35))
pos_ref = nothing

fig = Figure(size=(1050, 380))
for (k, (N_k, label)) in enumerate(nerves)
    M_k = classical_mapper(X, C_n, DBscan(radius=0.1), N_k)
    # Re-use Spring positions from the SimpleNerve graph for visual alignment
    pos_k = (k == 1) ? layout_spring(M_k) : layout_spring(M_k)
    isnothing(pos_ref) && (pos_ref = pos_k)
    sizes_k = rescale(map(length, M_k.C); min=10, max=50)
    vals_k  = node_colors(M_k, fv)

    n_edges = sum(length(v) for v in M_k.g.fadjlist) ÷ 2
    ax = Axis(fig[1, 2k-1], title=label * "\n($(length(M_k.C)) nodes, $n_edges edges)")
    sc = mapper_panel!(ax, M_k.g, pos_k, sizes_k, vals_k; colormap=:RdBu)
    Colorbar(fig[1, 2k], sc)
end
fig
```

### SimplicialNerve: beyond graphs

The `SimplicialNerve` builds the full simplicial complex: triangles appear when three nodes all mutually overlap.

```{julia}
M_sc = classical_mapper(X, C_n, DBscan(radius=0.1), SimplicialNerve(2))
SC   = M_sc.g
println("Vertices:  ", n_vertices(SC))
println("Edges:     ", n_edges(SC))
println("Triangles: ", n_triangles(SC))
```

---

## 9. Worked Example: Anatomy of a Torus {#sec-example}

We now build a full analysis of the torus, showing every visualization capability.

```{julia}
X_full = torus(2_000)
fv_z_full = [p[3] for p in X_full]

C_full = R1Cover(fv_z_full, Uniform(length=14, expansion=0.40))
M_full = classical_mapper(X_full, C_full, DBscan(radius=0.3))
println("Nodes: ", length(M_full.C), "  Edges: ", sum(length.(M_full.g.fadjlist)) ÷ 2)
```

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Torus anatomy: six views of the same mapper graph. Rows: metric layout (MDS) vs topology layout (Spring). Columns: coloured by x, y, z."

pos_mds_f  = layout_mds(M_full)
pos_spr_f  = layout_spring(M_full)
sizes_f    = rescale(map(length, M_full.C); min=8, max=42)

coords_f = [
    (node_colors(M_full, [p[1] for p in X_full]), :RdBu,   "x-axis"),
    (node_colors(M_full, [p[2] for p in X_full]), :plasma,  "y-axis"),
    (node_colors(M_full, fv_z_full),              :hsv,    "z-axis"),
]

fig = Figure(size=(1050, 700))
for (col, (vals_f, cmap, coord_label)) in enumerate(coords_f)
    # Row 1: MDS layout
    ax1 = Axis(fig[1, 2col-1], title="MDS layout · $coord_label")
    sc1 = mapper_panel!(ax1, M_full.g, pos_mds_f, sizes_f, vals_f; colormap=cmap)
    Colorbar(fig[1, 2col], sc1)

    # Row 2: Spring layout
    ax2 = Axis(fig[2, 2col-1], title="Spring layout · $coord_label")
    sc2 = mapper_panel!(ax2, M_full.g, pos_spr_f, sizes_f, vals_f; colormap=cmap)
    Colorbar(fig[2, 2col], sc2)
end
fig
```

---

## Reference {#sec-reference}

### IntervalCovers

| Type | Key params | Best for |
|------|-----------|----------|
| `Uniform(length, expansion)` | `length`, `expansion` | Default |
| `QuantileCover(n_intervals, expansion)` | `n_intervals` | Skewed data |
| `AdaptiveCover(n_intervals, expansion)` | `n_intervals` | Variable density |
| `LogarithmicCover(n_intervals, expansion)` | `n_intervals` | Log-scale data |
| `DyadicCover(max_depth, expansion)` | `max_depth` | Multiscale |
| `ManualCover(intervals)` | `intervals::Vector{Interval}` | Full control |

### Refiners

| Type | Key params | Notes |
|------|-----------|-------|
| `Trivial()` | — | No clustering |
| `DBscan(radius)` | `radius`, `min_neighbors` | Automatic k; recommended |
| `Hierarchical(linkage, threshold)` | threshold-based cut | `:single/:complete/:average/:ward` |
| `SingleLinkage(threshold)` etc. | convenience wrappers | |
| `FirstEmptyBin(num_bins)` | `num_bins` | Automatic cut |
| `KMeans(k)` | `k` | Euclidean |
| `KMedoids(k)` | `k`, `metric` | Metric-agnostic |
| `OPTICSRefiner(min_neighbors)` | `min_neighbors` | Variable-density |
| `SpectralRefiner(k, n_neighbors)` | `k`, `n_neighbors` | Non-convex clusters |

### Nerves

| Type | Constructor | Notes |
|------|------------|-------|
| `SimpleNerve()` | — | Default |
| `MinCountNerve(n)` | positional `Int` | Require n shared points |
| `PercentageNerve(p)` | positional `Float64` | Relative overlap |
| `JaccardNerve(threshold)` | positional `Float64` | Jaccard similarity |
| `SimplicialNerve(max_dim)` | positional `Int` (default 2) | Full complex |

### TDAplots functions

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `mapper_plot(M; ...)` | Full Figure from mapper |
| `node_colors(M, v; f=mean)` | Aggregate per-point values to nodes |
| `node_colors(M, v::Vector{String})` | Categorical node labels |
| `layout_spring(M)` | Force-directed layout |
| `layout_stress(M)` | Stress majorization |
| `layout_landmarks(M)` | Centroid-based (geometry-preserving) |
| `layout_mds(M)` | MDS on centroid distances |
| `layout_umap(M)` | UMAP on centroid distances |
| `layout_pca(M)` | PCA on centroid coordinates |
| `metricspace_plot(X; ...)` | Scatter of raw point cloud |
| `rescale(x; min, max)` | Rescale to [min, max] |

### Constructing a mapper

```julia
# Classical (filter-based)
M = classical_mapper(X, R1Cover(fv, Uniform(length=10, expansion=0.3)),
                     DBscan(radius=0.1), SimpleNerve())

# 2D filter
M = classical_mapper(X, f, Uniform(length=8, expansion=0.4),
                            Uniform(length=8, expansion=0.4))

# Ball mapper
M = ball_mapper(X, farthest_points_sample_ids(X, 80), 0.3)

# Generic (any combination)
M = mapper(X, cover, refiner, nerve)
```
